title: Risk Management
summary: Identifying, analysing, and responding to threats and opportunities so uncertainty is managed rather than merely feared.

sections.heading: Identifying risks & the Risk Breakdown Structure
sections.body:
A risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has an effect on one or more project objectives. Crucially, that effect can be negative (a threat) or positive (an opportunity) — PMI treats both as risk, so good risk management is not only about avoiding harm but also about capturing upside.

Risk identification is iterative and team-driven: it continues throughout the project, not just at the start, because new risks emerge as work progresses and old ones expire.

The Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) is a hierarchical chart of risk categories — not individual risks — used to prompt systematic identification so nothing obvious is missed. A typical top level is:
- Technical (requirements, technology, complexity, interfaces, quality)
- External (suppliers, regulation, market, weather)
- Organisational (resources, funding, priorities, dependencies)
- Project management (estimating, planning, controlling, communication)
sections.tip: RBS organises risk by SOURCE/category; the WBS organises deliverable work. Don't confuse them.

sections.heading: Qualitative analysis — the probability & impact matrix
sections.body:
Qualitative analysis rapidly scores and ranks each identified risk so attention goes to the high-priority few. You rate probability and impact (typically high/medium/low or a numeric scale) and multiply or plot them on a probability and impact matrix, often colour-coded red/amber/green.

The output is a prioritised list: red risks demand response now, green risks may go on a watch list. It is fast and subjective — it relies on expert judgement rather than hard numbers.

Qualitative analysis usually decides which risks warrant deeper quantitative analysis or an immediate response, and which can simply be monitored.
sections.tip: Qualitative = quick, subjective, prioritises. Think "which risks matter most?" before spending money modelling them.

sections.heading: Quantitative analysis — EMV & decision trees
sections.body:
Quantitative analysis is the numeric side: it assigns money or time figures to risk to model overall project exposure. Expected Monetary Value (EMV) = probability x impact, expressed in money — negative for threats, positive for opportunities. Summing EMVs gives an expected contingency figure.

Decision trees extend EMV across choices: each branch's EMV is calculated and you select the path with the best overall EMV. Other quantitative techniques include Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis (tornado diagrams).

Quantitative analysis is effort-intensive and is often skipped on smaller or agile projects, where qualitative ranking and adaptive responses are enough.
sections.tip: EMV of a 20% chance of a -£50k threat = -£10k. Threats carry a minus sign; opportunities a plus.

sections.heading: Risk responses — threats and opportunities
sections.body:
Each strategy for a threat has a mirror for an opportunity. Choose responses proportionate to the risk's priority.

Threats:
- Avoid — eliminate the threat entirely or remove its cause (e.g. change scope or approach)
- Transfer — shift the impact to a third party via insurance, contracts or warranties; the risk still exists, ownership of its consequence moves
- Mitigate — reduce the probability and/or the impact to an acceptable level
- Accept — take no proactive action; active acceptance sets aside a contingency reserve, passive acceptance simply deals with it if it occurs
- Escalate — the threat is outside the project's authority, so raise it to programme, portfolio or senior management

Opportunities:
- Exploit — make certain the opportunity happens (the mirror of avoid)
- Enhance — increase its probability and/or impact (mirror of mitigate)
- Share — allocate it to a partner best able to capture it, e.g. a joint venture or teaming agreement (mirror of transfer)
- Accept — take it if it arrives but don't actively pursue it
- Escalate — beyond the project's remit, so raise it upward
sections.tip: Transfer and Share both involve a third party. Escalate and Accept are the two strategies shared by threats and opportunities.

sections.heading: Secondary & residual risk, and the bowtie
sections.body:
Applying a response is not the end of the story:

- Residual risk is the exposure that REMAINS after a response is applied — you mitigated, but some risk is left. It is expected, and is then accepted or watched.
- Secondary risk is a NEW risk CREATED BY the response itself — e.g. transferring work to a subcontractor creates a delivery-quality risk. Always check what a response introduces.

The bowtie method visualises a serious risk: the central knot is the unwanted event, with threats (causes) on the left and consequences on the right. Preventive barriers (controls that stop the causes) sit on the left; recovery barriers (controls that limit the consequences) sit on the right. It is most useful for high-severity risks where you want to show prevention and mitigation at a glance.
sections.tip: Residual = left over after the response; Secondary = caused by the response. Bowtie: prevent on the left, recover on the right.

figure.caption: Bowtie: preventive barriers stop the causes (left); recovery barriers limit the consequences (right).

sections.heading: Reserves — contingency vs management
sections.body:
Two reserves cover two different kinds of uncertainty.

Contingency reserve covers KNOWN risks — the identified "known-unknowns" you analysed and chose to accept or mitigate. It is part of the cost baseline, and the project manager can spend it without further approval.

Management reserve covers UNKNOWN risks — the "unknown-unknowns" you could not foresee. It sits OUTSIDE the cost baseline (within the total project budget) and requires management approval to access.
sections.tip: Known + in baseline + PM controls = contingency. Unknown + outside baseline + needs approval = management reserve.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
